summary: Manage the routing layer: add and remove routes on route tables, repoint a route's target (forces replacement), retag route tables, associate a subnet with a route table, and move a subnet's association to a different route table.

operations.title: Add a route to a route table
operations.summary: Add a new route pointing at a gateway, NAT, or peering connection. A wrong destination can silently misroute traffic.
operations.description: Appends a new route {} block to an existing route table, pointing at a NAT gateway, internet gateway, transit gateway, peering connection, or ENI. An overlapping or wrong CIDR can silently blackhole traffic or leak it into the wrong network.

params.name: route_table
params.label: Route table
params.help: The route table to add the new route to.

params.name: destination_cidr
params.label: Destination CIDR

bounds.semantic: non-overlapping destination CIDR, not 0.0.0.0/0 unless replacing the default route

params.help: Destination network this route applies to, e.g. 10.2.0.0/16. An existing route for the same CIDR is never overwritten (BLOCK_EXISTS).

params.name: target_type
params.label: Target type
params.help: Which kind of target the route points at. An internet gateway is written to the provider's gateway_id attribute (there is no internet_gateway_id route attribute).

params.name: target_id
params.label: Target resource ID

bounds.semantic: must be a live resource matching the chosen target type

params.help: ID of the gateway, NAT, TGW, peering connection, or ENI to route to.

operations.title: Associate a subnet with a route table
operations.summary: Attach a subnet to this route table. All its resources switch to the new routes immediately, which can briefly interrupt traffic.
operations.description: Creates a new route table association so a subnet uses the selected table instead of the VPC main route table. Every resource in the subnet starts using the new routing behaviour immediately.

params.name: route_table
params.label: Route table
params.help: The route table the subnet will be associated with.

params.name: subnet_id
params.label: Subnet
params.help: Subnet whose traffic will be governed by this route table.

operations.title: Retag a route table
operations.summary: Add or update tags on this route table. Only the tags you list change — routes and subnet links are untouched.
operations.description: Adds or updates cost-allocation or environment tags on a route table; purely metadata, with no effect on routes or subnet associations. Only the keys you supply are changed — existing tags not listed here are left untouched.

params.name: route_table
params.label: Route table
params.help: Route table whose tags are updated, chosen from the estate inventory so only a Terraform-managed route table can be selected; retagging never alters routes or subnet associations.

params.name: new_tags
params.label: Tags
params.help: Key/value tags to merge onto the route table, e.g. Environment=prod, CostCentre=Platform. Only these keys are changed; existing tags not listed here are left untouched.

operations.title: Change a route's target
operations.summary: Repoint a route at a new target. Applying REPLACES the route — traffic on that address range drops briefly during the change.
operations.description: Repoints an existing route at a different NAT gateway, TGW, peering connection or gateway. In this estate's module structure this forces the route to be destroyed and recreated, so traffic on that CIDR drops for the duration of the apply.

params.name: route
params.label: Route
params.help: The route whose target is being changed.

params.name: new_target_type
params.label: New target type
params.help: Which Terraform target attribute the route will now set — NAT gateway, internet gateway, transit gateway, peering connection, or network interface; the allowlist is limited to these five supported next-hop kinds so no other target attribute can be authored through this form.

params.name: new_target_id
params.label: New target resource ID
params.help: AWS resource ID of the new next-hop, e.g. nat-0abc12345678 or tgw-0abc12345678; the pattern only accepts a well-formed ID with a recognised gateway/interface prefix, and it must identify a live resource matching the chosen target type.

bounds.semantic: must be a live resource matching the chosen target type

operations.title: Remove a route
operations.summary: Delete a route. Traffic for that address range immediately loses its path, unless a broader route can catch it.
operations.description: Deletes a route from a route table. Any traffic destined for that CIDR loses its path immediately and falls back to a less-specific route or nothing at all, so this needs a confirmed no-longer-needed CIDR before it runs.

operations.title: Move a subnet to a different route table
operations.summary: Move a subnet to a different route table, changing routing for every resource in it at once — high blast radius if routes mismatch.
operations.description: Repoints an existing subnet's route table association at a different table, changing the routing behaviour for every resource in that subnet at once. High blast radius if the destination table's routes don't match what the subnet's workloads expect.

params.name: new_route_table_id
params.label: New route table
params.help: Route table the subnet should be moved to.

operations.title: Provision a new route table
operations.summary: Request a brand-new, empty route table in a VPC; an engineer finalizes and reviews the Terraform before it applies.
operations.description: Stand up a new route table as a complete specification: which VPC it belongs to. Submitting routes it to an engineer with a draft Terraform block to review and finish — nothing is applied without review. It starts with only the VPC's implicit local route; routes and subnet associations are separate follow-up steps.

params.name: route_table_name
params.label: Route table name
params.help: Lower-case letters, numbers and dashes. Names the table in the generated draft — a route table has no name field of its own.

params.name: vpc
params.label: VPC
params.help: The VPC this route table belongs to.

params.name: resource_name
params.label: Route table name tag
params.help: Shown as the name everywhere — consoles, tickets, this portal.

params.name: description_tag
params.label: What this table is for
params.help: One line an operator can act on at 3 AM.

params.name: pic
params.label: Person in charge
params.help: Who to call about this table.

decisions: An empty route table has no effect until subnets are associated with it and routes are added — reachability is decided entirely by what is added afterward (the existing add-a-route and associate-a-subnet actions), not by creating the table itself
Route propagation from a VPN gateway (propagating_vgws) is an engineer decision
